body:
## Decision
approve

## Differentiation thesis
This is worth shipping if we position it as a manuscript completion system, not a generic task list: we unify scattered revision inputs (scene notes, beta feedback, pass outputs) into a single ordered queue tied to scene context and completion momentum. That is stronger than market parity and directly supports finishing drafts.

## Competitor evidence
- LivingWriter offers a To-Do List with task priority/due date and chapter/manuscript/global tabs, but it is still a task list surface rather than a dedicated revision execution queue with source-aware triage: https://guides.livingwriter.com/product-documentation/to-do-list
- LivingWriter Version History supports draft recovery/revert, which is useful but retrospective; it does not provide forward revision sequencing by severity/story order/quick wins: https://guides.livingwriter.com/desktop-app-+-web-version/version-history-1
- Inkitt recently added inline comments for line-level reader feedback and author replies, but this remains feedback capture, not integrated fix-queue execution: https://inkitt.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/articles/21022248999698-Inline-Feedback-on-Inkitt

## Risks / trade-offs
- Risk of becoming a me-too checklist if task fields are generic and not manuscript-specific.
- Queue complexity can slow drafting flow if always visible or too dense.
- Bulk actions (merge/snooze) can hide important issues if severity rules are weak.

## Recommendation
Proceed, but keep differentiation explicit in design/build: prioritize manuscript-native triage (scene-linked context, source-aware creation, revision momentum metrics) over generic PM-board patterns.

## Handoff
Assign to: Design
